name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَأَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُونِ (٩٢)
```

```english
Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me. (92)
```

```arabic
وَتَقَطَّعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلٌّ إِلَيْنَا رَٰجِعُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
They have fragmented their religion among themselves, but everyone of them will return to Us. (93)
```

```arabic
فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَا كُفْرَانَ لِسَعْيِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ كَـٰتِبُونَ (٩٤)
```

```english
Whoever is faithful and does righteous deeds, his endeavour shall not go unappreciated, and We will indeed record it for him. (94)
```

```arabic
وَحَرَٰمٌ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed [to return to the world]: they shall not return, (95)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنسِلُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
until when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they race down from every slope, (96)
```

```arabic
وَٱقْتَرَبَ ٱلْوَعْدُ ٱلْحَقُّ فَإِذَا هِىَ شَـٰخِصَةٌ أَبْصَـٰرُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا قَدْ كُنَّا فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا بَلْ كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٩٧)
```

```english
and the true promise draws near [to its fulfillment], behold, the faithless will look on with a fixed gaze: ‘Woe to us! We have certainly been oblivious of this! Indeed, we have been wrongdoers!’ (97)
```

```arabic
إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنتُمْ لَهَا وَٰرِدُونَ (٩٨)
```

```english
Indeed you and what [idols] you worship besides Allah shall be fuel for hell, and you will enter it. (98)
```

```arabic
لَوْ كَانَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ءَالِهَةً مَّا وَرَدُوهَا ۖ وَكُلٌّ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
Had they been gods, they would not have entered it, and they will all remain in it [forever]. (99)
```

```arabic
لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
Their lot therein will be groaning, and they will not hear anything in it. (100)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ (١٠١)
```

```english
Indeed those to whom there has gone beforehand [the promise of] the best reward from Us will be kept away from it. (101)
```

```arabic
لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا ۖ وَهُمْ فِى مَا ٱشْتَهَتْ أَنفُسُهُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
They will not hear even its faint sound and they will remain [forever] in what their souls desire. (102)
```

```arabic
لَا يَحْزُنُهُمُ ٱلْفَزَعُ ٱلْأَكْبَرُ وَتَتَلَقَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (١٠٣)
```

```english
The Great Terror will not upset them, and the angels will receive them [saying]: ‘This is your day which you were promised.’ (103)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ نَطْوِى ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَطَىِّ ٱلسِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأْنَآ أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُّعِيدُهُۥ ۚ وَعْدًا عَلَيْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ (١٠٤)
```

```english
The day We shall roll up the heaven, like rolling of the scrolls [meant] for writings. We will bring it back just as We began the first creation—a promise [binding] on Us. [That] indeed We will do. (104)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِى ٱلزَّبُورِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلذِّكْرِ أَنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِىَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’ (105)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى هَـٰذَا لَبَلَـٰغًا لِّقَوْمٍ عَـٰبِدِينَ (١٠٦)
```

```english
There is indeed in this a proclamation for a devout people. (106)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٧)
```

```english
We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations. (107)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٠٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So will you submit?’ (108)
```

```arabic
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ ءَاذَنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۖ وَإِنْ أَدْرِىٓ أَقَرِيبٌ أَم بَعِيدٌ مَّا تُوعَدُونَ (١٠٩)
```

```english
But if they turn away, say, ‘I have proclaimed to you all alike, and I do not know whether what you have been promised is far or near. (109)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْجَهْرَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْتُمُونَ (١١٠)
```

```english
Indeed He knows whatever is spoken aloud, and He knows whatever you conceal. (110)
```

```arabic
وَإِنْ أَدْرِى لَعَلَّهُۥ فِتْنَةٌ لَّكُمْ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (١١١)
```

```english
I do not know—maybe it is a trial for you and an enjoyment for a while.’ (111)
```

```arabic
قَـٰلَ رَبِّ ٱحْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَرَبُّنَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ (١١٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Judge [between us and the polytheists] with justice.’ ‘Our Lord is the All-beneficent; [He is our] resort against what you allege.’ (112)
```

## (Commentary)

In these verses, there is a return to the opening of the discourse. It has been previously explained that for humanity, there is only one God, and He is the One who originated the heavens and the earth. Therefore, they must worship Him through the path of prophethood (النبوة) and by responding to its call, thereby preparing themselves for the reckoning of the Day of Accounts. Prophethood has never invited to anything but a single religion (الدين), which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — just as Moses called to it before, and before him Abraham, and before him Noah, and those who came after Moses and before Noah among those whom Allah, the Glorified, alluded to by name, mentioning a portion of the favors He bestowed upon them, such as Job, Idris, and others.

Thus, humanity is but a single community (أمة واحدة) with one Lord, who is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), and one religion, which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), wherein Allah alone is worshipped. This was the aim of the divine call. However, people "cut up" their affair among themselves, becoming divided in their religions and inventing for themselves deities besides Allah and religions other than the religion of Allah. Consequently, their states differed, and the ultimate ends of their journeys in this world and the Hereafter diverged.

As for the Hereafter, the righteous (الصالحين) among them will find that Allah will appreciate their striving (سعيهم), they shall not witness what grieves them, and they shall remain in bliss and honor. As for others, their destination is punishment and retribution.

As for this world, Allah has promised the righteous (الصالحين) among them that He will cause them to inherit the earth and grant them the ultimate outcome of the Abode, while the wicked are headed toward destruction, ruin, loss, and futility.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ** — *Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me.* (21:92). The term "community" (الأمة) refers to a group united by a single objective. The address in the verse—as evidenced by the context of the verses—is a general address encompassing all legally responsible individuals (المكلفين) among humans. What is meant by "community" is the human species, which is a single species. The feminine demonstrative pronoun in His saying "this" (هذِهِ) is used because the predicate (الخبر) is feminine.

The meaning is: This human species is your community, O assembly of humans, and it is a single community; and I—Allah, the One, exalted is His Name—am your Lord, for I possess you and govern your affairs; therefore, worship Me and none other.

In His saying, "one community" (أُمَّةً واحِدَةً), there is an allusion to the proof for the command to worship Allah, the Glorified. Since the human species is a single species and a single community with a single objective—which is the happiness of human life—it cannot have but one Lord. This is because Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية) are not honorary, conventional positions (مناصب تشريفية وضعية) such that a human may choose for himself whatever he wishes, however many he wishes, or in whatever manner he wishes. Rather, they are ontological principles (مبدئية تكوينية) for the governance of his affairs. Since the human is a single specific reality (حقيقة نوعية واحدة), and the system governing his affairs is a single, continuous system whose parts are interconnected, a single system of governance can only be maintained by a single Governor. Thus, it makes no sense for humans to differ in the matter of Lordship, such that some take a lord other than what others take, or one group follows a path in His worship different from what others follow. The human is a single species and must take a single Lord, who is the Lord by the reality of Lordship (بحقيقة الربوبية), and He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

It has been said that "community" (الأمة) here means religion (الدين), and the demonstrative "this" refers to the religion of Islam, which was the religion of the prophets. The meaning of it being "one community" would then be the gathering or consensus of the prophets upon it. The meaning would be: The creed of Islam is your creed, the boundaries of which you must preserve, and it is a creed upon which the prophets (peace be upon them) agreed.

However, this is far-fetched. Even if the use of "community" (الأمة) to mean religion were permissible, it would be a metaphorical usage (تجوز) that cannot be reached except through a diverting context (قرينة صارفة). There is no reason to divert from the literal meaning (المعنى الحقيقي) when it is sound, upright, and supported by other words of the Exalted, such as His saying: **وَمَا كَانَ النَّاسُ إِلَّا أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَاخْتَلَفُوا** — *Mankind were but a single community; then they differed* (10:19). This verse, as you see, contains in summary what this verse and the one following it contain in detail.

Furthermore, the expression in His saying, "and I am your Lord" (وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ), using "Lord" (الرب) instead of "God" (الإله), would remain without justification according to their interpretation. This is in contrast to taking "community" to mean a group, for the meaning then is: You are a single species and I am the Owner and Governor of your affairs; so worship Me so that you may take Me as a God (إلها).

There are many other views mentioned regarding the verse, but they are all far from the context. We have omitted them; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the more extensive commentaries.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلٌّ إِلَيْنا راجِعُونَ** — *They fragmented their affair among themselves; [but] they will all return to Us.* (21:93). "Fragmentation" (التقطع), according to what is stated in *Majma' al-Bayan*, carries the meaning of "cutting up" (التقطيع), which is division. It is also said to carry its primary meaning, which is divergence and disagreement. The word "their affair" (أَمْرَهُمْ) is in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition (بنزع الخافض), the implied meaning being "they fragmented *in* their affair." It is also said that "fragmented" (تَقَطَّعُوا) implies the meaning of "making" (الجعل), which is why it takes a direct object.

In any case, His saying, "They fragmented their affair among themselves" is a metaphor by way of metonymy (استعارة بالكناية). The meaning is that they took this single affair—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) called for through prophethood—and turned this unified matter into fragmented pieces, distributing it among themselves. Each group took a portion of it and left another, such as the idolaters, the Jews, the Christians, the Magians, and the Sabians, despite their various sects. This is a form of rebuke for the people and a condemnation of their disagreement in religion and their abandonment of the divine command to worship Him alone.

His saying, "they will all return to Us" (كُلٌّ إِلَيْنا راجِعُونَ) explains that their disagreement in the matter of religion will not be left in vain without consequence. Rather, they will all return to Allah and be recompensed according to their disagreements, as alluded to by the detail mentioned in His subsequent saying: "So whoever does righteous deeds..." etc.

The separation (الفصل) of the sentence "they will all return to Us" is because it functions as an answer to an implied question, as if it were asked: "Where does their disagreement in the matter of religion end? And what does it produce?" It was then said: They will all return to Us, and We shall recompense them according to what they did.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلا كُفْرانَ لِسَعْيِهِ وَإِنَّا لَهُ كاتِبُونَ** — *Whoever does righteous deeds, should he be a faithful, his endeavor shall not go unappreciated, and We will indeed record it for him.* (21:94). This is a detailed explanation of the state of those who differed in terms of their otherworldly recompense. The detail regarding their recompense in this world will come in His saying: **وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِن بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ** — *Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’* (21:105).

His saying, "So whoever does righteous deeds" (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِنَ الصَّالِحاتِ) means whoever among them performs any of the righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحات). He conditioned the performance of some righteous deeds upon faith (الإيمان) when He said: "should he be a faithful" (وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ), for a righteous deed has no effect without faith.

What is meant by faith—as appears from the context, especially His saying in the previous verse: "and I am your Lord, so worship Me"—is faith in Allah specifically. However, faith in Allah is inseparable from faith in His prophets without exception, due to His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ... أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْكَافِرُونَ حَقًّا** — *Those who deny Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and deny others’... it is they who are truly faithless.* (4:150-151).

His saying, "his endeavor shall not go unappreciated" (فَلا كُفْرانَ لِسَعْيِهِ) means there shall be no covering up (ستر) of the righteous deeds he has performed. "Ingratitude/denial" (الكفران) is the opposite of "appreciation/gratitude" (الشكر); hence, this meaning is expressed elsewhere by His saying: **وَكَانَ سَعْيُكُم مَّشْكُورًا** — *and your endeavor has been appreciated.* (76:22).

His saying, "and We will indeed record it for him" (وَإِنَّا لَهُ كاتِبُونَ) means We record it in the scrolls of deeds (صحائف الأعمال), a recording that precludes forgetting. Thus, the meaning of "his endeavor shall not go unappreciated, and We will indeed record it for him" is that his righteous deed shall neither be forgotten nor denied.

This verse is among those indicating that the acceptance of righteous deeds is conditional upon faith, as supported by the verses regarding the nullification of deeds (حبط الأعمال) in the state of disbelief. It also indicates that a believer who performs some righteous deeds is among those who will be saved.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَحَرامٌ عَلى قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها أَنَّهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ** — *It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed [to think] that they shall not return.* (21:95). What first comes to mind from the verse, aided by the context of the detailed explanation, is that the meaning is that the people of the town We have destroyed shall not return a second time to this world to obtain the blessing of life they lost or to make up for the righteous deeds they missed. This constitutes one side of the detailed explanation, the other side of which was contained in His saying: "So whoever does righteous deeds, should he be a faithful..." etc. Thus, the other side of the detail is that he who was not a believer and performed righteous deeds has no recorded deed or appreciated endeavor; rather, he is disappointed and lost, his endeavor having gone astray in this world, and he has no way to a second life in this world to rectify what he missed.

However, the Exalted placed the community (المجتمع) in the place of the individual when He said: "It is forbidden for [the people of] any town that We have destroyed," and He did not say: "It is forbidden for those whom We have destroyed." This is because the corruption of the individual naturally spreads to the community and ends in their rebellion (طغيانهم), whereby the word of punishment becomes due upon them and they are destroyed, as He said: **وَإِن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوهَا عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا** — *There is no town but We shall destroy it before the Day of Resurrection, or punish it with a severe punishment.* (17:58).

It is possible—though far-fetched—that "destruction" (الإهلاك) refers to destruction through sins, in the sense of the nullification of the capacity for happiness and guidance, as in His saying: **وَإِن يُهْلِكُونَ إِلَّا أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ** — *they destroy none but themselves, and they are not aware.* (6:26). The verse would then carry the meaning of His saying: **فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي مَن يُضِلُّ** — *Allah does not guide those whom He leads astray.* (16:37). The meaning would be: It is forbidden for a people whom We have destroyed through their sins and decreed misguidance upon them to return to repentance and a state of uprightness.

The meaning of the verse is: For the town that did not perform righteous deeds while being faithful, and whose affair resulted in destruction, it is impossible for them to return and rectify the appreciated endeavor and the recorded, accepted deed they missed.

As for His saying, "that they shall not return" (أَنَّهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ), while the apparent expectation was to say "that they shall return," the truth is that this is a rational trope (مجاز عقلي). In it, the result of the action's attachment to something—meaning what the state of the object becomes after the action attaches to it—is placed in the position of the object itself. The result of the attachment of "prohibition" (الحرمة) to their "return" is "non-return." Thus, this result was placed in the position of the "return" itself, which is the object of the prohibition. In this construction, there is an indication of the effectiveness of the action, as if the return, by the mere attachment of the prohibition, becomes a non-return without any intervening gap.

A similar instance is His saying: **مَا مَنَعَكَ أَلَّا تَسْجُدَ إِذْ أَمَرْتُكَ** — *What prevented you from prostrating, when I commanded you?* (7:12), where the attachment of "prevention" (المنع) to the "prostration" results in the "absence of prostration." Thus, the absence of prostration, which is the result, was placed in the position of the prostration itself, which is the object of the prevention.

Another similar instance is His saying: **قُلْ تَعَالَوْا أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئًا** — *Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: that you shall not ascribe any partners to Him’* (6:151), where the attachment of "prohibition" (التحريم) to "shirk" (الشرك) results in the "absence of shirk." Thus, the absence of shirk, which is the result, was placed in the position of the shirk itself, which is the object. We have previously explained these two verses with another interpretation as well.

The commentators have several other interpretations for the verse:

Among them: that "not" (لا) is extra (زائدة), and the original meaning is "that they shall return."

Among them: that "forbidden" (حرام) carries the meaning of "obligatory" (واجب), i.e., it is obligatory upon a town We have destroyed that they shall not return. They cited as evidence for "forbidden" meaning "obligatory" the saying of al-Khansa:

*And indeed, it is a duty (حرام) that I do not see time weeping*
*Over a grief, except that I weep for Sakhr.*

Among them: that the object of the prohibition is omitted, the implied meaning being: It is forbidden for a town We have destroyed through sins—meaning We found them destroyed by them—that any deed be accepted from them, because they do not return to repentance.

Among them: that what is meant by "non-return" is the lack of return to Allah, the Glorified, through resurrection, not the lack of return to this world. The meaning—based on the literal wording—would be: It is impossible for a town We have destroyed due to the rebellion of its people that they should *not* return to Us for recompense. You are aware of the weakness in each of these views.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّى إِذا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنْسِلُونَ** — *until Gog and Magog are let loose, and they surge from every mound.* (21:96). A "mound" (الحدب) is an elevation of the earth between depressions. "Surging/hastening" (النسول) is to exit with speed; from it comes the "hastening of the wolf." The context requires that His saying "until..." be the limit (غاية) for the detailed explanation mentioned in His saying: "So whoever does righteous deeds..." until the end of the two verses, and that the plural pronoun refers to Gog and Magog.

The meaning is: The matter will continue to proceed in this manner—We record the righteous deeds for the believers and appreciate their endeavor, and We destroy the unjust towns and forbid their return after destruction—until the time when Gog and Magog are "opened," meaning their barrier or their blocked path. They—Gog and Magog—will emerge toward the rest of the people from the elevations of the earth, rushing toward them. This is among the conditions of the Hour (أشراط الساعة) and the signs of the Resurrection, as He alludes to in His saying: **فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا * وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَاهُمْ جَمْعًا** — *But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it to the ground, and the promise of my Lord is true. On that day We shall leave them to surge against one another, and the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall gather them all together.* (18:98-99). We have fully discussed the meaning of Gog and Magog and the barrier built against them in the commentary on Surah al-Kahf.

It has been said that the plural pronoun refers to the people, and what is meant is their emergence from their graves to the land of gathering (المحشر).

However, the context before the sentence "until..." and after it "And the true promise has drawn near" does not suit this meaning. The same applies to the sentence itself in terms of it being a circumstantial clause (حال). Furthermore, "hastening from every mound"—which the sentence contains—does not apply to the emergence from graves. For this reason, the proponent of this view, Mujahid, read it as "the graves" (الجدث) with the letters *jim* and *tha*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاقْتَرَبَ الْوَعْدُ الْحَقُّ فَإِذا هِيَ شاخِصَةٌ أَبْصارُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** — *And the true promise draws near: behold, the eyes of the faithless will be fixedly open!* (21:97). What is meant by the "true promise" is the Hour. The "fixing" (شخوص) of the eye is its staring such that the eyelids do not blink; this was mentioned by al-Raghib. It is a necessary consequence of the observer's total preoccupation with what he is looking at, such that he is not distracted by anything else, and it usually occurs during an evil that appears to a person suddenly.

His saying: **يا وَيْلَنا قَدْ كُنَّا فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا** — *‘Woe to us! We were certainly oblivious of this.’* (21:97) is a narration of the words of the disbelievers when they witness the Hour suddenly. They will call out woe upon themselves, claiming they were oblivious to what they are witnessing, as if they were made oblivious. Then they will turn away from that by confessing that the obliviousness only arose from their wrongdoing (ظلمهم) through preoccupation with worldly matters that cause one to forget the Hereafter and be oblivious to it. Thus they say: **بَلْ كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ** — *‘Rather, we were wrongdoers.’* (21:97).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ** — *Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell, and you will enter it.* (21:98). "Fuel" (الحصب) is that which is used to kindle fire; it is also said to mean firewood, and it is said its origin is whatever is thrown into the fire, making it more general.

What is meant by His saying "and what you worship besides Allah" (وَما تَعْبُدُونَ) — and He did not say "and *whom* you worship" (وَمَن تَعْبُدُونَ), despite the Exalted's usual reference to idols using terms specific to rational beings, as in His subsequent saying "they would not have entered it" (ما وَرَدُوها) — are the idols and statues they used to worship, excluding those worshipped among the prophets, the righteous, and the angels, as has been said. This is indicated by His subsequent saying: "Indeed those for whom the [promise of] the best reward has already gone forth from Us..." etc.

It appears that these verses are among the addresses of the Day of Resurrection to the disbelievers, containing the decree of their entry into the Fire and their eternity therein. They are not merely a report in this world of what will happen to them in the Hereafter, nor an argument for the falsehood of idol worship and their taking of gods besides Allah.

His saying, "and you will enter it" (أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ): the preposition *lam* is for emphasizing the transitivity or carries the meaning of "to." The apparent context is that the address includes both the disbelievers and the deities together; i.e., you and your deities will enter Hell or be brought to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَوْ كانَ هؤُلاءِ آلِهَةً ما وَرَدُوها وَكُلٌّ فِيها خالِدُونَ** — *Had these been gods, they would not have entered it; and they will all remain in it forever.* (21:99). This is a derivation and a disclosure of the reality of the state of the deities they used to worship so that they might be intercessors for them. His saying, "and they will all remain in it forever" means everyone among you and the deities.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَهُمْ فِيها زَفِيرٌ وَهُمْ فِيها لا يَسْمَعُونَ** — *There will be groaning for them in it, and they will not hear [anything] in it.* (21:100). "Groaning" (الزفير) is the sound of drawing the breath inward; hence it has been interpreted as the braying of a donkey. Their not hearing is a recompense for their not hearing the word of truth in this world, just as they do not see as a recompense for their turning away from looking at the signs of Allah in this world.

In the verse, there is a shift (عدول) from addressing the disbelievers to addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), turning away from addressing them in order to explain their evil state to others. Accordingly, the plural pronouns refer to the disbelievers specifically, not to them and the deities together.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ** — *Indeed those for whom the [promise of] the best reward has already gone forth from Us, they will be kept away from it.* (21:101). "The best reward" (الحسنى) is the feminine of "the best" (أحسن), and it is an adjective standing in place of its noun. The implied meaning is the "best promise" (العدة أو الموعدة الحسنى) of salvation or Paradise. The promise of both is mentioned in the words of the Exalted; He said: **ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا** — *Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees.* (19:72), and He said: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ جَنَّاتٍ** — *Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens...* (9:72).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَها... تُوعَدُونَ** — *They will not hear its sound... you were promised.* (21:102-103). "Sound" (الحسيس) is the sound that is sensed. The "Great Terror" (الفزع الأكبر) is the greatest fear. The Glorified has informed of its occurrence during the blowing of the Trumpet, where He said: **وَيَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ** — *The day the Trumpet is blown, everyone who is in the heavens and everyone who is on the earth will be struck with terror.* (22:87).

His saying: "and the angels will receive them" (وَتَتَلَقَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ) means with glad tidings, which is their saying: "This is your day which you were promised."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ** — *The day We shall roll up the sky like the rolling of the scroll for the writings. We will bring it back just as We began the first creation.* (21:104) until the end of the verse. It is stated in *al-Mufradat*: "The scroll (السجل): it is said it was a stone upon which writing was done, then everything written upon was called a scroll. The Exalted said: 'like the rolling of the scroll for the writings,' i.e., like its rolling of what is written in it to preserve it." End quote. This is the clearest and simplest meaning said regarding this word.

Based on this, His saying "for the writings" (لِلْكُتُبِ) is the object of "rolling," just as "the scroll" is its subject. The meaning is that the scroll—which is the page in which the book is written—when it is rolled up, the book is rolled up with its rolling. The book consists of the words or meanings that have a type of realization and stability in the scroll by means of lines and inscriptions. Thus, the book disappears thereby, and no trace or essence of it remains visible. Similarly, the sky is rolled up by divine power, as He said: **وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ** — *and the heavens will be rolled up in His right hand.* (39:67). Thus, it disappears from others, and no essence or trace of it remains visible, except that it does not disappear from the Realm of the Unseen (عالم الغيب), even if it disappears from others, just as the book does not disappear from the scroll even if it disappears from others.

The rolling of the sky, according to this, is its return to the treasuries of the Unseen (خزائن الغيب) after it had descended from them and been measured, as the Exalted said: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** — *There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure.* (15:21). And He said generally: **وَإِلَى اللَّهِ الْمَصِيرُ** — *and to Allah is the return.* (3:28). And He said: **إِنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعَىٰ** — *Indeed, the return is to your Lord.* (96:8).

Perhaps in view of this meaning, it was said that His saying: "just as We began the first creation, We will bring it back" (كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ) looks toward the return of everything to the state it was in when its creation began, which is that it was not a thing mentioned, as the Exalted said: **وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْئًا** — *I created you before when you were nothing.* (19:9). And He said: **هَلْ أَتَىٰ عَلَى الْإِنسَانِ حِينٌ مِّنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُن شَيْئًا مَّذْكُورًا** — *Has there been a period of time for man when he was not anything worthy of mention?* (76:1).

This is the meaning of what is attributed to Ibn Abbas: that the meaning of the verse is that everything perishes as it was the first time. Although this is suitable for connection with His saying: "The day We shall roll up the sky..." etc., to serve as its justification, the predominant context of the preceding verses is the explanation of "bringing back" (الإعادة) in the sense of returning things after their annihilation, not "bringing back" in the sense of annihilating things and returning them to their state before their appearance in existence.

Thus, the apparent context of the verses is that the meaning is: We shall resurrect the creation just as We began it. The *kaf* in His saying "just as We began..." is for comparison, the *ma* is infinitive-forming, and "the first creation" is the object of "We began." The meaning is that We shall bring back the creation like its beginning in ease, without it being difficult for Us.

His saying: **وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ** — *[This is] a promise binding on Us; [and] We will indeed do it.* (21:104) means We have promised it a promise that We have made binding upon Ourselves, and it is necessary for Us to fulfill it; and We are indeed doers of what We promised, for that is Our sunnah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ** — *Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’* (21:105). It appears that what is meant by "the Psalms" (الزبور) is the book of David (peace be upon him), and it has been called by this name in His saying: **وَآتَيْنَا دَاوُدَ زَبُورًا** — *and We gave David the Psalms.* (4:163, 17:55). It has been said that it refers to the Quran, and it has been said it refers to all the books revealed to the prophets, or to the prophets after Moses, but there is no evidence for any of that.

Regarding "the Torah/Remembrance" (الذكر), it has been said it is the Torah, and Allah has called it by this name in two places in this Surah: His saying, "So ask the People of the Remembrance if you do not know" (verse 7) and His saying, "and a reminder (ذكراً) for the God-conscious" (verse 48). It has also been said it is the Quran, and Allah has called it "Remembrance" in several places in His word; the Psalms being "after" the Remembrance according to this view would be a rank-based posteriority, not a temporal one. It has also been said it is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), which is as you see.

His saying: "Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth" (أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ): "Inheritance" (الوراثة والإرث), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the transfer of a possession to you without a transaction.

What is meant by the inheritance of the earth is the transfer of authority over its benefits to them and the establishment of the blessings of life therein among them. These blessings are either worldly, pertaining to the life of this world—such as the righteous enjoyment of its goods and ornaments—in which case the import of the verse is that the earth will be purified from polytheism and disobedience, and a righteous human society will inhabit it, worshipping Allah and not ascribing any partner to Him, as alluded to by His saying: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ... يَعْبُدُونَنِي لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئًا** — *Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth... They will worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me.* (24:55).

Or they are otherworldly, which are the stations of proximity they earned in their worldly life, for they are among the blessings of earthly life and are the bliss of the Hereafter, as alluded to by His saying, narrating from the people of Paradise: **وَقَالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنَا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشَاءُ** — *They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inherit the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’* (39:74), and His saying: **أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْوَارِثُونَ * الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ** — *It is they who will be the inheritors, who shall inherit paradise.* (23:10-11).

From here it becomes clear that the verse is general (مطلقة), and there is no reason to restrict it to one of the two inheritances as some have done. They are divided between those who restrict it to the otherworldly inheritance, holding fast to the verses that suit it—and they might argue for its necessity by saying that the previous verse mentions the "bringing back" (resurrection), and there is no earth after the resurrection for the righteous to inherit; this is refuted by the fact that the verse being conjoined to the previous one is not certain, for it is possible it is conjoined to His earlier saying: "So whoever does righteous deeds..." as we shall point out.

And between those who restrict it to the worldly inheritance and apply it to the time of the appearance of Islam or the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), which was reported by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in mutawatir narrations (الأخبار المتواترة) — *narrations transmitted by so many chains that their authenticity is certain* — related through the paths of both sects, and they hold fast to the verses suitable for that, some of which we have alluded to.

In short, the verse is general, encompassing both inheritances. However, what is required by consideration of the context is that it be conjoined to His earlier saying: "So whoever does righteous deeds, should he be a faithful..." etc., which alludes to the detail of the state of those who differed in the matter of religion in terms of otherworldly recompense. This verse would then allude to their detail in terms of worldly recompense. The sum total would be: We commanded them with one religion, but they fragmented and differed; thus, Our recompense for them differed. As for the Hereafter, the believers have an appreciated endeavor and a recorded deed, while the disbelievers have the opposite. As for this world, the righteous have the inheritance of the earth, unlike others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ فِي هذا لَبَلاغاً لِقَوْمٍ عابِدِينَ** — *There is indeed in this a message for a worshipping people.* (21:106). "Sufficiency/Message" (البلاغ) is that which is sufficient, and also that by which one reaches a goal, and also the reaching itself. The meaning of the verse is sound according to each of the three meanings. The demonstrative "this" refers to the knowledge (المعارف) explained in the Surah.

The meaning is: In what We have explained in the Surah—that the Lord is one, there is no lord besides Him, He must be worshipped through the path of prophethood, and one must prepare thereby for the Day of Reckoning, and that the recompense of the believers is such-and-forth and the recompense of the disbelievers is thus-and-so—there is sufficiency for a worshipping people; if they take it and act upon it, it will suffice them and they will reach their goal thereby.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** — *We did not send you but as a mercy to all the nations.* (21:107). That is, you are a mercy sent to all human groups—the proof for this is the plural noun defined by the article *al*—and that is necessitated by the universality of the message.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a mercy for the people of the world in terms of his bringing a religion in the adoption of which lies the happiness of the people of the world in their worldly life and their Hereafter.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a mercy for the people of the world in terms of the good effects that spread from his establishing the true call in their societies, which appears with great clarity when comparing general human life today to what it was before his mission (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and applying one life to the other.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنَّما يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** — *Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So will you submit?’* (21:108). That is, what is revealed to me of the religion is nothing but monotheism (التوحيد) and what branches out from it and resolves into it, whether it be a creed or a ruling. The proof for what we have mentioned is the occurrence of restriction upon restriction (الحصر على الحصر) and its appearance as a real restriction (حصر حقيقي).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ آذَنْتُكُمْ عَلى سَواءٍ** — *But if they turn away, say, ‘I have proclaimed [the truth] to you all alike...’* (21:109). "Proclaiming" (الإيذان)—as has been said—is the causative form of "permission" (الإذن), which is the knowledge of authorization in something and its licensing. Then it was used metaphorically for absolute knowledge, and verbs were derived from it. It often implies the meaning of warning and cautioning.

His saying: "all alike" (عَلى سَواءٍ) is apparently a circumstantial clause (حال) for the object of "I have proclaimed to you." The meaning is: If they turn away from your call and turn away from submitting to Allah through monotheism, then say: I have informed you that you are in danger of it, because you are equal in the information or in the danger. It has been said: I have informed you of war, but this is far-fetched in a Meccan Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ أَدْرِي أَقَرِيبٌ أَمْ بَعِيدٌ ما تُوعَدُونَ إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما تَكْتُمُونَ** — *‘...and I do not know whether what you are promised is near or far. Indeed He knows what is spoken aloud, and He knows what you conceal.’* (21:109-110). This is the completion of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanded speech.

What is meant by "what you are promised" is what His saying "I have proclaimed to you all alike" alludes to regarding the threatened punishment. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded, first, to inform them of the danger if they turn away from Islam, and second, to negate from himself the knowledge of the proximity or distance of its occurrence, attributing this to the fact that knowledge is limited to what is spoken aloud of their words—which is their slandering of Islam and their public mockery—and what they conceal of that from Allah, the Glorified, for He is the Knower of the reality of the matter.

From this, it is known that the source of the punishment directed toward them is what they used to slander Islam with in public and what they harbored of plotting. It is as if it were said: They deserve punishment for their public speech regarding this divine call and their private plotting against it; thus He threatened them with it. However, since you do not encompass the outward of their speech and the inward of their plotting, and you do not know the extent to which their crime necessitates punishment in terms of the proximity or distance of the term, then negate the knowledge of the specifics of its proximity and distance from yourself and return the knowledge of that to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

It has thereby been known that what is meant by "what is spoken aloud" is what the polytheists manifested of speech regarding Islam by way of slander and mockery, and by "what they conceal" is what they harbored against it of plotting and deception.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ أَدْرِي لَعَلَّهُ فِتْنَةٌ لَكُمْ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ** — *‘And I do not know; maybe it is a trial for you and an enjoyment for a while.’* (21:111). This is from the completion of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanded speech. The pronoun in "maybe it is" (لَعَلَّهُ), as has been said, is a metonymy for something not mentioned; perhaps it refers to the commanded proclamation. The meaning is: I do not know; perhaps this proclamation I was commanded with—meaning His, the Exalted, intent in His command to me to inform of the danger—is a trial for you to manifest what is in your hearts regarding the matter of the call. Thus, He intends thereby to test you and grant you enjoyment for a time and a term, by way of gradual enticement (استدراج) and respite.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ احْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَرَبُّنَا الرَّحْمنُ الْمُسْتَعانُ عَلى ما تَصِفُونَ** — *He said, ‘My Lord! Judge with justice! Our Lord is the All-beneficent, whose help is sought against what you allege.’* (21:112).

The pronoun in "He said" (قالَ) refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The verse is a narration of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) words regarding their call to the truth and their rejection of it and turning away from it. It is as if he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), when he called them and conveyed to them what he was commanded to convey, and they denied and were harsh in it, turned away from them to his Lord, turning in repentance to Him, and said: "My Lord! Judge with justice!" The qualification of the judgment with "justice" (بالحق) is explanatory, not restrictive, for His judgment, the Exalted, can only be just. It is as if it were said: My Lord, judge with Your just judgment. What is meant is the manifestation of the truth for whoever it was for and against whoever it was against.

Then he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned to them and said: "Our Lord is the All-beneficent, whose help is sought against what you allege." It is as if he is alluding thereby to the reason for his turning away from them and returning to Allah, the Glorified, and his asking Him to judge with justice. For He, the Glorified, is his Lord and the Lord of them all; thus, it is for Him to judge between His subjects. He is the One of abundant mercy (الرحمن) who does not disappoint the one who asks Him and turns to Him. He is the One who judges, and there is no reversing His judgment; He is the One who establishes the truth and nullifies falsehood by His words. Thus, he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his word "My Lord! Judge with justice!" is returning to the One who is his Lord and their Lord, asking Him by His mercy to judge with justice, and seeking His help against the falsehood they allege, which is their describing their religion with what is not in it and their slandering the true religion with what it is innocent of.

From what has preceded, some of the nuances in the vocabulary of the noble verse have become clear, such as the shift (الالتفات) from address to the third person in "He said," and the reference to Him, the Exalted, first as "my Lord" and second as "our Lord," and His description as "the All-beneficent" and "the One whose help is sought," among other things.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَرامٌ عَلى قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها** (the verse): Muhammad ibn Muslim narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said: "Every town that Allah destroyed with a punishment—they shall not return."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "When this verse was revealed—meaning His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ** (Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell)—the people of Mecca were deeply distressed by it. Then Abdullah ibn al-Ziba'ra entered upon them while the disbelievers of Quraysh were discussing this verse. Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: 'Did Muhammad speak this verse?' They said: 'Yes.' Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: 'If he admits to it, I shall surely overcome him in argument.' So they were brought together.

He said: 'O Muhammad, tell me about the verse you recited just now—is it regarding us and our deities specifically, or all nations and their deities?' He replied: 'Rather, it is regarding you and your deities, and all nations and their deities—except those whom Allah has exempted.' Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: 'I have overcome you, by Allah! Do you not speak well of Jesus? And you know that the Christians worship Jesus and his mother, and that a group of people worship the angels—are these not in the Fire with the deities?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'No.' Quraysh then raised a clamor and laughed, saying: 'Ibn al-Ziba'ra has overcome you.' The Messenger of Allah said: 'You have spoken falsehood. Did I not say: "except those whom Allah has exempted"? And that is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى ـ أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ لا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَها ـ وَهُمْ فِي مَا اشْتَهَتْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ خالِدُونَ** (Indeed those for whom the [promise of] the best reward has already gone forth from Us, they will be kept away from it. They will not hear its sound... and they will remain forever in what their souls desire).'"

I say: The hadith has also been narrated through Sunni paths, but the text in this path is more solid than what was reported through their paths and more sound. That which is from Ibn Abbas says: "When **إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ ـ أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ** was revealed, it was hard upon the people of Mecca, and they said: 'He has insulted the deities.' Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: 'I shall overcome Muhammad for you; call him for me.' So he was called and he said: 'O Muhammad, is this thing for our deities specifically or for everyone worshipped besides Allah?' He said: 'Rather, for everyone worshipped besides Allah.' Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: 'I have overcome you, by the Lord of this Structure (meaning the Kaaba). Do you not claim, O Muhammad, that Jesus was a righteous servant, and Ezra was a righteous servant, and the angels are righteous?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then these Christians worship Jesus, and these Jews worship Ezra, and these Banu Malih worship the angels.' The people of Mecca raised a clamor and rejoiced.

Then there was revealed: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى** —Ezra, Jesus, and the angels— **أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ**. And there was revealed: **وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلاً إِذا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ** (And when the son of Mary was cited as an example, behold, your people raised a clamor at it)."

In this text, there are several issues. First: the mention of the name Ezra (عزير), while the event was in the early days of the mission in Mecca, and his name was not mentioned in any of the Meccan Surahs; it was only mentioned in Surah al-Tawbah, which is among the last to be revealed in Medina.

Second: his saying: "and these Jews worship Ezra," while the Jews do not worship Ezra; they only said "Ezra is the son of Allah" as an honorific, just as they said "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones."

Third: what it contains regarding the revelation of His saying: "Indeed those for whom the [promise of] the best reward has already gone forth from Us, they will be kept away from it" after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) admission of the generality of His saying: "Indeed you and what you worship" to every worshipped being besides Allah, and Ibn al-Ziba'ra's refutation of that using Jesus, Ezra, and the angels. This would be an instance of the explanation coming after the time of need, and it would strongly support the occurrence of the accusation.

Fourth: its inclusion of the revelation of His saying: "And when the son of Mary was cited as an example..." (the verse) in the event, while its content has no connection to it at all.

Similar to this is what is common among them that Ibn al-Ziba'ra objected to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with that, and he said to him: "O youth, how ignorant you are of the language of your people! For I said: 'what you worship' (ما تعبدون), and *ma* is for what does not possess intellect, and I did not say: 'whom you worship' (من تعبدون)."

In this, there is the flaw of what is attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of his saying: "I said such-and-so and I did not say such-and-so." It is necessary to exalt the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) above uttering regarding a Quranic verse something like "I said such-and-so and I did not say such-and-so." It was narrated from al-Hafiz ibn Hajar that the hadith has no basis and was not found in any of the books of hadith, neither *musnad* nor otherwise.

Similar in weakness is what was reported in another hadith narrating this story, that Ibn al-Ziba'ra said: "Did you say that?" He said: "Yes." He said: "I have overcome you, by the Lord of the Kaaba! Did not the Jews worship Ezra, and the Christians worship the Messiah, and the Banu Malih worship the angels?" He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Rather, they worshipped the devils who commanded them to do that." Then Allah revealed: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى** (the hadith).

This is because the argument attributed to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the hadith benefits Ibn al-Ziba'ra more than it harms him. For the argument, just as it excludes Ezra, Jesus, and the angels from the scope of the verse, it also excludes the deities that are idols, for they share with those mentioned the fact that they have no knowledge of the worship of their worshippers nor any pleasure in it, as they have no consciousness. Thus, the verse would be restricted to the devils and would not include the idols, which is contrary to what was attributed to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of the claim that the verse encompasses their deities and his confirming it.

Similar in weakness is what is in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from al-Bazzar from Ibn Abbas, who said: "This verse was revealed: **إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ ـ أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ**, then it was abrogated (نسخها) by His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى ـ أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ**."

The reason for the weakness is apparent; if there were anything there, it would be specification (تخصيص), not abrogation.

In the *Amali* of al-Saduq, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a hadith: "O Ali, you and your Shi'a will be at the Pond (الحوض)—you will give drink to whom you love and prevent whom you dislike—and you are the secure ones on the Day of the Great Terror in the shade of the Throne. The people will be terrified and you will not be terrified, and the people will grieve and you will not grieve. Regarding you this verse was revealed: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى ـ أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ**, and regarding you was revealed: **لا يَحْزُنُهُمُ الْفَزَعُ الْأَكْبَرُ ـ وَتَتَلَقَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ هذا يَوْمُكُمُ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ**."

**I say:** The meaning of its being revealed regarding them is its application (جريها) to them or their inclusion among those regarding whom it was revealed. Many narrations have been reported regarding a group of believers who were counted among those to whom the two verses apply, especially the second, such as the one who recites the Quran seeking reward, and leads a people in prayer seeking reward, and a man who gives the call to prayer seeking reward, and a slave who fulfills the right of Allah and the right of his masters; this was narrated in *al-Majma'* from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Also, those who love one another for the sake of Allah, those who travel in the darkness [to the mosque], and the emigrants (المهاجرين); *al-Durr al-Manthur* narrated the first from Abu al-Darda, the second from Abu Umamah, and the third from al-Khudri, all from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In the hadiths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), a vast number of people have been counted among those to whom the verse applies.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd ibn Humayd reported from Ali regarding His saying: **كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ**: "He said: An angel."

**I say:** It was also narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Asakir from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) in a hadith.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: "As for His saying: **يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ**, he said: 'The scroll (السجل) is the name of the angel who rolls up the books, and the meaning of his rolling them up is his annihilating them—so they turn into smoke and the earth into fires.'"

In *Nahj al-Balagha*, describing the dead: "They exchanged the surface of the earth for its belly, and spaciousness for narrowness, and family for estrangement, and light for darkness. So they came to it as they left it, barefoot and naked—they have journeyed from it with their deeds to the eternal life and the lasting abode—as the Glorified said: **كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ ـ وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ**."

**I say:** His (peace be upon him) citation of the verse is applicable to both meanings of "bringing back" (الإعادة), namely, the return of creation to what they began from, and the return of creation in the sense of their being brought to life after their death as they were before their death. Both meanings have preceded in the explanation of the verse.

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "You shall be gathered on the Day of Resurrection barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised— **كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُة وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ**."

**I say:** A similar narration was reported in *Nur al-Thaqalayn* from the book of al-Durusti with his chain from Ibn Abbas from him (ص).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: "And His saying: **وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ**, he said: 'All the books are Remembrance (الذكر).' **أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ**, he said: 'The Qāʾim and his companions.' He said: 'And in the Psalms (الزبور) are prophecies (ملاحم), praises, glorifications, and supplications.'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding the Mahdi (peace be upon him), his appearance, and his filling the earth with equity and justice after it has been filled with injustice and oppression, from the paths of both the general and the specific [Sunni and Shi'a] from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), reach the level of *tawātur*. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to their respective sources in the books of both groups.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bayhaqi reported in *al-Dala'il* from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I am but a gifted mercy.'"
